metadata.summary: Ad testing methodology: pre-register hypothesis, isolate single variable, compute sample size, run control vs. variant with equal budget, verdict at 95% confidence.

content.title: A/B Testing Ads
content.summary: Scientific method applied to ad creative: pre-register one hypothesis (one variable changed), calculate required sample size before launching, run control vs. variant on the same audience with equal budget, and only verdict once 95% confidence is reached. Test in priority order: offer → hook/headline → creative type → visual style → copy length → CTA. Document every result to build an institutional learning library.

why.p: Without controlled tests, every campaign iteration is a guess. You cannot learn whether your blue button beats red, or whether testimonials outperform product shots, if you change multiple things at once. Pre-registering hypothesis and sample size prevents peeking errors and p-hacking. A learning library compounds: each test narrows the hypothesis space for the next, accelerating improvement.

list.item: Running a continuous testing cadence across hooks, creatives, and offers.
Evaluating a specific creative hypothesis before scaling spend.
Coordinating cross-platform tests (Meta + Google) on the same hypothesis.
Maintaining a tests registry for team knowledge sharing.
Daily/weekly conversion volume below 100 per variant — tests will never reach significance; ship the better-reasoned option and move on.
Brand-new accounts with under 30 days history — algorithmic variance dominates creative variance.
Holiday peaks (BFCM, Q4 retail) — exogenous demand swamps treatment effect; run proven creatives.
Tests where multiple variables changed — use multivariate or Bandit design instead.

section.name: testing-priority

rule.statement: Test elements in this order: offer first, then hook/headline, then creative type, then visual style, then copy length, then CTA last.
rule.rationale: Higher-priority elements have larger effect sizes. Offer changes can produce 2-5x differences; CTA wording changes produce 5-15% differences. Testing CTA before offer wastes budget on low-leverage experiments when the offer may be the fundamental problem.

section.p: Frequency by element: Offer → monthly; Hook/headline → bi-weekly; Creative type → monthly; Visual style → monthly; Copy length → as needed; CTA → quarterly.

rule.statement: Change exactly one element per test; hold audience, budget, placement, optimization goal, and all other creative elements constant.
rule.rationale: If you change hook AND visual simultaneously and the variant wins, you cannot attribute the improvement to either element. You have wasted the test — you know the combined package is better, but you cannot apply the learning to future tests because you do not know which element drove it.

antipattern.description: Running a "fresh creative" test where headline, visual, copy, and format are all different between control and variant.
antipattern.reason: This is not a test — it is a coin flip between two complete creative packages. Neither outcome produces actionable learning about which element to iterate on.

section.name: sample-size-requirements

rule.statement: Require 1,000+ impressions per variant for CTR tests; require 100+ conversions per variant for CPA tests; calculate minimum detectable effect before launching.
rule.rationale: Below these thresholds, variance dominates signal. A variant with 1.5% CTR from 300 impressions has a 95% confidence interval spanning 0.6%-2.4% — the result is meaningless. Pre-computing sample size sets the test duration and prevents premature stopping.

section.p: Minimum confidence thresholds: 95% for decisions that change account structure or kill a creative; 90% for directional learning; 80% for quick iteration when speed matters and the cost of a wrong call is low.

example.note: Quick significance check before declaring a winner.
example.code:
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 1.2% (variant), +20% lift → Needs more data — could be noise at typical sample sizes.
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 1.5% (variant), +50% lift → Likely significant with 1,000+ impressions each.
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 2.0% (variant), +100% lift → Almost certainly significant; confirm with calculator.

section.name: test-structure
section.p: Standard structure for manual tests: one campaign, one ad set (same audience for both), control ad and variant ad within that ad set. Use Meta's Experiments tool when budget allows (200+ per variant) — it guarantees equal traffic split; default ad-set rotation does not.

rule.statement: Pre-register every test: document hypothesis, sample size, primary metric, decision rule, and stop date before launching. No retroactive metric switching.
rule.rationale: Retroactive metric switching (looking at 10 metrics and declaring a winner on whichever looks best) is p-hacking. Pre-registration creates accountability and prevents motivated reasoning when results are disappointing.

section.name: meta-experiments
section.p: Meta's Experiments tool (Ads Manager → Experiments → A/B Test) guarantees equal traffic split. Supports testing: audiences, creatives, placements, optimization goals. Minimum 200/variant. Meta declares a winner automatically when significance is reached.

rule.statement: Use Meta's Experiments tool for all creative tests when budget allows; default ad rotation does not guarantee equal split and will bias results toward the algorithm's early favorite.
rule.rationale: Meta's algorithm begins optimizing within hours of launch. Without the Experiments tool, the platform reallocates spend toward whichever variant performs better early, breaking the equal-split assumption that makes frequentist analysis valid.

section.name: google-ads-experiments
section.p: Google Ads Experiments (Campaigns → Experiments → Ad Variations): create a variation, set traffic split percentage (50/50 for clean test), set duration, monitor, apply winner. For search ads: test one ad element at a time within an ad group. Review after statistical significance is reached in platform reporting.

section.name: bayesian-verdict
section.p: For off-platform analysis, use Bayesian Beta-binomial testing: P(variant > control) > 0.95 = winner; less than 0.05 = control wins; between 0.05 and 0.95 = inconclusive, extend the test or accept the uncertainty.

antipattern.description: Mixing significance methods — using Meta's platform significance for some tests and scipy.stats.ttest_ind for others.
antipattern.reason: Different statistical engines compute significance differently. Mixing methods makes tests incomparable and can produce contradictory verdicts on the same data.

rule.statement: Never auto-promote a variant to winner without human sign-off, even when the significance threshold is reached.
rule.rationale: A variant may win on CTR while losing on CPA. The agent's job is to compute and report the verdict; the human decides whether to apply it and which metric matters most for the business decision.

list.item: Require sample size computation before launching any test; refuse to verdict until threshold is met.
When variant outperforms by more than 50%, check for data leakage (audience overlap, tracking gap) before reporting as a winner.
Inconclusive tests must extend, not restart — restarting resets the sample and wastes the data already collected.
Cross-currency or cross-region tests need revenue normalized to a base currency before computing ROAS lift.
Running 5+ concurrent tests on the same audience inflates false positive rate — apply Bonferroni correction or limit concurrent tests to 3 per audience.

template.purpose: Test brief: hypothesis, variables, success metric, parameters, expected outcome
Results template: winner, confidence, lift, data table, learnings, next steps
Quarterly testing roadmap with monthly test schedule and backlog
Bayesian Beta-binomial verdict function (p_variant_better, expected lift, winner)

section.name: agentic-workflow-and-best-practices
section.p: The testing agent runs three roles: (1) test designer — reads a hypothesis backlog (YAML), checks prerequisites (budget, sample-size feasibility, no overlapping tests on same audience), and writes a test brief; (2) launcher — creates matched ad sets via Marketing API with locked parameters and a test_id tag; (3) analyst — pulls insights daily, computes significance using a pre-committed method (Bayesian or fixed-horizon), and emits a verdict report. Humans approve the brief and the verdict-to-promotion step. The agent never auto-promotes a winner without sign-off.

rule.statement: Pre-register every test: hypothesis, sample size, primary metric, decision rule, stop date. No retroactive metric switching.
rule.rationale: Pre-registration creates accountability and prevents motivated reasoning when results are disappointing.

rule.statement: Track holdout cells (5-10% of spend) on proven creatives to detect platform-wide drift versus test-specific regression.
rule.rationale: Holdout benchmarks prevent false victories that are actually platform-wide improvements unrelated to the variant.

rule.statement: Keep a tests registry table: (test_id, hypothesis, control_id, variant_id, status, started_at, verdict, learning). Feed it back into the brainstorm loop.
rule.rationale: A registry compounds learning across the organization and prevents repeated testing of the same hypothesis.

rule.statement: Cap concurrent tests per audience to avoid overlap. A user in 3 tests at once is a confound, not a sample.
rule.rationale: Concurrent tests on the same audience inflate false positive rate without Bonferroni correction.

section.p: Recommended subagents: faion-ads-agent (Marketing API ad set / experiment CRUD), faion-sdd-executor-agent (wraps each test as an SDD task), brainstorm (generates hypothesis backlog from past test learnings + competitor ad library scrape).

antipattern.description: LLMs eagerly declare winners on small samples.
antipattern.reason: Always require the agent to compute sample size first and refuse to verdict until threshold met.

antipattern.description: "Statistical significance" in Meta UI is not always the same metric as scipy.stats.ttest_ind on raw counts.
antipattern.reason: Pin one stats engine and do not mix. Different statistical engines compute significance differently; mixing methods makes tests incomparable.

antipattern.description: When variant outperforms by more than 50%, suspect data leakage (audience overlap, tracking gap) before celebrating.
antipattern.reason: Have agent run an integrity check (impression counts within 5% of expected split).
The variant might win on CTR but lose on CPA. Require no regression on secondary metric beyond X%.

antipattern.description: Agents recreate a "test" each week even when last week's winner was inconclusive.
antipattern.reason: Enforce: inconclusive tests extend, not restart. Restarting resets the sample and wastes data already collected.
Agent must convert revenue to a base currency before computing ROAS lift.

section.name: platform-differences-and-limitations
section.p: Meta and Google's auto-optimization will reallocate spend mid-test, breaking the equal-split assumption. Methodology says "equal budget split" — only Meta's Experiments tool guarantees this; default ad-set rotation does not.
"Stop early when significance hits" is a peeking error in classical NHST. Use sequential testing (mSPRT, Bayesian) or pre-commit to a sample size.
Sample-size calculators assume one test at a time. Running 5 concurrent tests inflates false positives 5x without correction (Bonferroni or BH-FDR).
Conversion metrics with long lag (B2B 30-90d) cannot be tested at ad level — by the time signal arrives, creatives are stale.
Platform attribution windows differ (Meta 7d-click, Google 30d-click); naive cross-platform comparison fabricates lifts.

how-to.p: When running a testing cadence with agents:
how-to.step: Test designer role — read hypothesis backlog (YAML), check prerequisites (budget, sample-size feasibility, no overlapping tests on same audience), write test brief.
Launcher role — create matched ad sets via Marketing API with locked parameters and a test_id tag.
Analyst role — pull insights daily, compute significance using a pre-committed method (Bayesian or fixed-horizon), emit verdict report.
Humans approve the brief and the verdict-to-promotion step. Agents never auto-promote without sign-off.
